hash_format_notice: Manifest hash placeholders in this corpus are synthetic, derived deterministically. Top-level hash fields (tally_algorithm_hash, eligibility_snapshot_hash, action_set_hash, tally_input_hash, tally_output_hash, result_hash) use the seed pattern SHA-256 of 'actproof.testvector.ai_act.art26.<tv_id>.<field>'. Attachment hashes use the seed pattern SHA-256 of 'actproof.testvector.ai_act.art26.<tv_id>.attachment.<label>'. Compact note r and e values use the same seed pattern with field names compact_note.r and compact_note.e, raw bytes base64url-encoded without padding. The vector ids in the seed strings are the short forms tv01, tv02, tv03. The attachment seed convention here is explicit (label-keyed) and differs from the NIS2 Article 20 corpus convention; both conventions are documented per-file in this notice. Hashes have the correct cryptographic shape (32-byte SHA-256, hex or base64url-encoded) but are not computed from canonical manifest inputs. Corpus v1.1 will replace these with reproducible hashes computed from documented canonical inputs in a companion test-vectors/inputs/ directory. The catalogue-conformance verification path exercised by these vectors is independent of the cryptographic verification path and does not require resolved inputs.
verifier_result_code_notice: Test vectors 2 and 3 use result codes FAIL_MANIFEST_METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED and FAIL_MANIFEST_QUORUM_BELOW_THRESHOLD that are proposed for inclusion in v1.4 of the master spec amendment, not present in v1.3. Master spec amendment v1.3 section 9.6 defines PASS_WITH_SEMANTICS as requiring the manifest to conform to the catalogue entry's required fields and method constraints, but does not define explicit failure codes for the negative cases. Implementations against v1.3 may report these failures as PASS_WITH_SEMANTICS with a structured conformance result field, or as an unstructured fail. Implementations against v1.4 are expected to use the explicit codes.

test_vectors.description: An AI Act Article 26 deployer compliance attestation by the AI governance committee of a fictitious Bulgarian bank deploying a high-risk AI credit scoring system, using simple majority voting with the catalogue minimum quorum, with all required manifest fields present and the four substantive attachments included. Expected to produce PASS_WITH_SEMANTICS.
test_vectors.scenario_narrative: On 10 September 2026 at 14:00 UTC, the AI governance committee of Acme Finance Bulgaria approved the deployer compliance attestation under AI Act Article 26 for the bank's deployment of a high-risk AI credit scoring system. Credit scoring of natural persons falls under Annex III paragraph 5(b) of Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 and is therefore classified as high-risk. The committee has 9 members (CTO, CRO, CCO, CDO, head of credit, head of legal, head of compliance, head of audit, head of risk operations). The vote used simple_majority_v1 with quorum at 5001 basis points (more than 50% of committee members present) and threshold at 5001 basis points (more than 50% of votes cast in favour). All four required attachments are present: the deployer's risk assessment document, the Article 13 instructions for use received from the AI system provider, the human oversight assignment record per Article 26(2), and the data protection impact assessment under GDPR Article 35 per Article 26(8).
test_vectors.rationale: All thirteen required_manifest_fields are present in the manifest. The method_id simple_majority_v1 is in the allowed_method_ids list. The quorum_basis_points value of 5001 meets the catalogue minimum of 5001 (the boundary case). The decision_type is regulatory_attestation, the tenant_id and system_created_at are present, and four attachment_hashes entries are present including the load-bearing risk_assessment_document and the article_13_instructions_for_use that AI Act Article 26 substantively requires. The compact note carries the act-type identifier in disclosed mode with version, type, merkle root, envelope hash, manifest count, and s field all conforming to section 9.2 of the amendment. Under v1.3 section 9.6 semantics, the verifier returns PASS_WITH_SEMANTICS.

test_vectors.description: A claimed AI Act Article 26 deployer compliance attestation using a method (plurality_v1) that is not in the catalogue entry's allowed_method_ids list. The cryptographic proof path is intact and the s field is well-formed, but the manifest fails catalogue conformance. Expected to produce FAIL_MANIFEST_METHOD_NOT_ALLOWED (proposed v1.4 code).
test_vectors.scenario_narrative: The same AI governance committee attempts to record an attestation using plurality_v1 (first-past-the-post without majority requirement) rather than one of the four approval-family methods the catalogue entry permits. Plurality without majority does not satisfy the substantive requirement that the deployer organisation's designated governance body collectively approves the compliance attestation. AI Act Article 26 places obligations on the deployer as a whole; a committee plurality where a minority position prevails does not constitute a deployer-level attestation in the meaningful sense.
test_vectors.rationale: All required fields are present, the s field is well-formed, and the catalogue entry resolves. The single point of failure is that the manifest's method_parameters.method_id of plurality_v1 is not in the catalogue entry's allowed_method_ids list. The check_performed list does not include quorum_basis_points_at_or_above_minimum because the method check fails first and downstream checks are short-circuited in this corpus's verifier model. This negative case demonstrates that the catalogue entry encodes the substantive regulatory requirement (collective deployer attestation by an approval-family method) as a mechanically-checkable constraint. The expected_combined_verifier_result is proposed for v1.4 of the master spec amendment.

test_vectors.description: A claimed AI Act Article 26 deployer compliance attestation using an allowed method (simple_majority_v1) but with a quorum threshold of 4000 basis points, below the catalogue entry's minimum_quorum_basis_points of 5001. Expected to produce FAIL_MANIFEST_QUORUM_BELOW_THRESHOLD (proposed v1.4 code).
test_vectors.scenario_narrative: The same AI governance committee attempts to record an attestation where the decision-rules quorum is 40% of the committee. AI Act Article 26 places obligations on the deployer as a whole, and the implicit requirement is that the deployer's designated governance body collectively determines that appropriate technical and organisational measures are in place. A 40% quorum rule means the decision could be valid with only four of nine committee members present, which does not satisfy the substantive requirement of collective governance-body attestation. The catalogue entry encodes this floor as minimum_quorum_basis_points = 5001.
test_vectors.rationale: All required fields are present, the s field is well-formed, the catalogue entry resolves, and the method id is in the allowed list. The single point of failure is that the manifest's method_parameters.quorum_basis_points of 4000 falls below the catalogue entry's minimum_quorum_basis_points of 5001. This negative case demonstrates that the catalogue entry encodes the substantive regulatory requirement (collective deployer governance body, more than half present) as a numeric constraint, and that a verifier reading the catalogue entry can mechanically detect non-conformance even when the cryptographic proof path is otherwise valid. The expected_combined_verifier_result is proposed for v1.4 of the master spec amendment.

gaps_surfaced_for_v1_4_amendment.description: The catalogue entry can only allow voting methods drawn from the master spec's method registry, but real-world AI Act Article 26 compliance attestations are often made by a single designated AI governance officer (small or mid-sized deployers without committee structures). The method registry does not yet include a designated_officer_attestation_v1 or single_signer_approval_v1 method, and the catalogue entry schema cannot express such methods even if they existed. v1.4 should extend the method registry with non-voting attestation methods and extend the catalogue entry schema's allowed_method_ids semantics to accommodate them.

AI Act Article 26(8) requires deployers to use the Article 13 information to comply with their DPIA obligation under GDPR Article 35, but only 'where applicable'. The catalogue entry can either require a DPIA attachment unconditionally (which over-constrains the rule) or omit any DPIA expectation (which under-constrains). Similar conditional structures appear in Article 26(7) (worker information, only when deployer is an employer) and Article 26(8) (DPIA, only where GDPR Article 35 applies). v1.4 should add a conditional_required_fields block with predicate language, mapping to manifest field paths that become required when the predicate evaluates true.

AI Act Article 26 deployer attestations are closely related to Article 27 fundamental rights impact assessments (FRIAs) for specific deployer categories (public-law bodies, providers of public services, certain Annex III categories). The catalogue entry has no field for cross-citing related act types. v1.4 should add a related_acts list pointing to other catalogue entries that the verifier may want to load for context, with a relationship qualifier (requires, supplements, supersedes, references). Same gap was noted for NIS2 Article 20 against possible related Article 21 and Article 32 entries.

The regulatory_citation block has no field for the unambiguous CELEX identifier (for example, 32024R1689 for the AI Act, 32022L2555 for NIS2). CELEX is the canonical EU regulatory identifier and is useful for stable URL construction and disambiguation across instruments with ambiguous short forms. v1.4 should add an optional regulatory_citation.instrument_celex field that conformant catalogues populate for EU instruments.

The regulatory_citation block has no field for the canonical EUR-Lex ELI URL (for example, https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj). Useful for receipt renderers that want to link directly to the authoritative regulatory text. v1.4 should add an optional regulatory_citation.instrument_url field with similar precedents for non-EU jurisdictions.

The catalogue entry has no top-level license_id field. The repository-wide LICENSE file at root declares Apache-2.0 for catalogue content, which is sufficient for v1, but REUSE-compliant repositories prefer per-file SPDX identifiers. v1.4 should add an optional top-level license_id field, defaulting to Apache-2.0 when absent.

The catalogue entry has no top-level created_at field recording when the entry was first published. The git commit log records this externally but is not portable across repository migrations. v1.4 should add an optional top-level created_at field in ISO 8601 date format.

The maintainer field is currently a single string. As the catalogue grows and multiple organizations co-maintain entries, a structured form with organization, contact, and (optionally) role becomes useful. v1.4 should allow maintainer to be either a string (current v1 form) or an object with fields organization, contact, role for backward-compatible extension.
